speaker: interviewee
text: [TEST PATTERN BEEPING]

speaker: interviewer
text: The day is February 20, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: I keep it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Leon Borovick is being interviewed. His name at birth was Leyb Borowick. The interviewer is Diane Weinreich, and the interview is taking place in the Bronx, New York, in USA. And the interview is being conducted in English. My name is Diane Weinreich, W-E-I-N-R-E-I-C-H. I'm interviewing Mr. Leon Borovick in his home in the Bronx, New York. The interview is being conducted in English. Today is February 20, 1997. Please state your name for us and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon Borovick, L-E-O-N B-O-R-O-V-I-C-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth, and spell it, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I cannot spell it. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: With a--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Leyb, Leybl, Leybl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leyb. And the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Borowick, with a-- with a W.

speaker: interviewer
text: With a W instead of a V.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In Polish, a W is pronounced like a V in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot. 84, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: 84. What is the city of your birth, the city and country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Porzecze, near Grodno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Porzecze, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-O-R-Z-E-C-Z-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in your family? Please identify--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Necha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Necha? How-- how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: N-E-C-H-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sisters or brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was with me together. My brother was-- his name is Moishe. Is he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Moishe. Is he-- was he older than you or younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Younger than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Younger than you. And who else was in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had three stepbrothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any-- any whole sisters? Any full sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a sister. I had a sister too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister perished in camp. Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sarah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And please tell us your earliest memories of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: What you remember about your family. Your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father died when I was a year and a half old, he drowned. Year and a half old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the circumstances?

speaker: interviewee
text: Half and a year-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: A year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a year and a half old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Years old, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and what were the circumstances? How did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the-- in-- in-- in-- in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the war. So you never really remember him.

speaker: interviewee
text: My actual birth was in 1912.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My actual birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was in 1912.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in Germany I made myself younger in order to survive. So I switched-- I came to America, I thought I had to transfer the same-- the same date. Officially, I am 1914. My father died when I was a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: She remarried.

speaker: interviewer
text: She remarried when you were how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. About six or five. I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name, your stepfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judel, Judel, Jude, Judel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jude, his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judah, I don't know. Segal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he had children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oy. Do I have to give all these things? The-- the-- the oldest was Motke and then Hirshke, Awremel, Schlemke, and a sister, Necha.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS] And what was life in your little town like?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was very young, we were very, very poor, extremely poor. Very young. But then-- then my mother managed to [INAUDIBLE] us. We-- we did make a living. But I-- I was in cheder, in-- in an old-fashioned school until 12-- 12 years of age I didn't have any secular education. We had-- we had only cheder, we had only arithmetic. I remember Russian a little. 12-- when I was 12 years old, I-- I was in second grade, only in a secondary-- an elementary school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that unusual for a Jewish child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was unusual? No, it was not. It was unusual for the-- in-- for the-- for the-- as a Polish citizen it was unusual. The Polish people, when they were 12 years old, they-- they used to be already in the seventh grade or the 15th grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did--

speaker: interviewee
text: But Jewish people were in cheder. They didn't go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was that-- was that common for Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish children did not attend--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't go. We went to cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish children did not attend--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, the public-- the public schools. But then it was compulsory. So we had to go. So I went-- I went to the 12th-- the 12th grade. And I left my little town. I went to a little-- little town, Jeziory. [INAUDIBLE] And now you learn privately. And I-- I entered the Zionist school. It was called [POLISH], [POLISH]. I went in the seventh grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: About 14 years old, I think. I don't remember where. I was there for a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have any-- any contact with non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only in public school when I was there for less than a year in-- in my little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that the first opportunity you had to be with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Very fast, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to be with non-Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: --children?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went to-- in to that [POLISH]. We had only-- only Jewish children, Zionist school. The language and instruction was Hebrew. All subjects were on Hebrew except for Polish and, I think, in Polish history or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your-- did your sister-- did your brother attend with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother went to yeshiva for a short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you expect that you would be able to do as you grew up with that kind of an education? What-- what kind of plans did your mother have for you? Were there any?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, certainly. I-- my mother sent me to a business school. I was up-- I graduated. I was out two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is in Polish, called [POLISH], you call it [POLISH] school. To be a bookkeeper, actually, towards a diploma. When I graduated, I was doing some bookkeeping, but I was fired. I was not a-- I was a lousy bookkeeper. It was not my-- my-- my talent for that. I didn't have a talent for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when-- when you started work as a bookkeeper? Were you just a teen--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. 17 or 16? I don't-- I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother earned a livelihood. How did she do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did many things. She was-- she had-- she was-- she used to, you know, dye the garments in like a-- it's not painting. You know, I told you about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: She dyed the-- the fabrics.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Fabrics for the peasants. And then she opened a store. And she did that. She did that. She also was-- she was also a baker. She baked bread and she sold it in the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did your stepfather do?

speaker: interviewee
text: A blacksmith.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did things get better once they were married? Were things easier in your household financially?

speaker: interviewee
text: Financially, it was-- we managed somehow to live, but my stepfather was not a-- the relations between us and the stepfather was not-- not a close relationship. But his-- his-- his children were very close to us. And I wrote a poem about these three brothers, my three stepbrothers who all died in-- in Auschwitz. They were like my brothers. The same thing. We had the same feeling like it was my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- you wrote a poem in tribute to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- yeah. There were three brothers there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of it that you'd like to recite for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that poem? Yeah. I have-- have to get it. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Afterwards? OK, we'll hear your poetry afterwards then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe I remember it by heart, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: You do remember it by heart?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think so. I'll-- I'll try.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Should I-- should I recite now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: [POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. I'm mixed up. OK. What-- what-- which you're paying--

speaker: interviewee
text: If I can take this-- can I take this for-- I have the poem. Should I read it?

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we'll-- we'll wait because--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, a little bit later, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you have the-- because you have the wires.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mixed up the brothers. The whole thing. I have to read, later, the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But you-- but you did-- but you did care for these brothers a great deal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much, yeah, very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- how did your family observe Jewish customs? How-- how did you live as Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my stepfather died, we-- my mother was traditional. She was religious, but not-- not-- not-- not too pious-- not too religious. I mean, not fanatic. She-- she only used to go to shul about twice a year or three times, that's all. When we-- when we were very later, when we were all grownups already-- I was 18, 19-- we seldom went to shul. For the-- the high holidays. Only in [INAUDIBLE], maybe. But then I became-- I became a teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: You became a teacher. Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was a-- a private teacher. I was in-- in-- in-- in 1937. In '38, in a little town of Sidra, near-- also not that far from Bialystok. Sidra. I had a-- I was teaching about nine or 10 girls the Hebrew language. It was a Zionist group. And I also asked private-- private lessons. That's how I made my-- made my living. And that's where I met my-- my actual wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Bashe. She was called Bashe. That's how-- that's how she is in my poems.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vinick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she a student of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a Zionist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Interesting. I-- I-- I was the first-- the first Zionist in my little town when I was very young. Then-- then I wasn't a happy Zionist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did you learn about Zionism? From whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- from reading. From-- to ask-- sure, that was in the air. The idea of-- of-- there was Keren Kayemet, Jewish National Fund. So I used to get out-- to-- to collect money for the-- for that fund. I didn't get paid for that. I used-- it was in every house there was a pushka, you know, that little-- and we used to-- and then we used to go every week or so and collect the money. That was a-- that was a tradition. It was not Zionism. It was-- that was Jewish life, a tradition to-- to-- to-- for Israel, for Zion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have plans to go to-- to Israel? To Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Unfortunately, I didn't have any plans then to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you felt there should be a Jewish state? Is that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Even--

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you were young?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believed in Zionism, that's right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Yeah, yeah, for me too. But see, to-- that would be to be uprooted from my-- my town. We didn't think in these terms. We just-- we were trying to help-- to do-- to help that cause. But that [INAUDIBLE] didn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you think-- who did you think the land of Israel would be for? Was there any connection with--

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew about that Dr. Herzl, we heard about this. I used to-- that's how I started writing poems. I-- I published my first five poems in Philadelphia. I sent from Poland in '98. In '98, I sent five poems to-- and they published it in a-- in a Jewish newspaper. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. Over

speaker: interviewer
text: But when-- when you were thinking of the land of Palestine--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was this in connection with-- with the rise of Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was separate.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you become aware of-- of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hitler was not in the picture then, when I started to-- to work for Keren Kayemet, for the Jewish National Fund. It was later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. What-- what-- so what year would it have been, about what year would it have been, when you were working for the Jewish National Fund?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about maybe, I don't know, 17 years old or-- or 19. I can't-- I don't remember. I cannot say right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so it was before-- it was before Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that the-- the feelings of-- of building a homeland for the Jews had nothing to do with-- with the rise of Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did it stem from? Why did you feel that Jews needed a homeland?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, because to me-- see, the motive to me to collect money for-- for-- for Keren Kayemet, that-- that was the thing to do. We didn't-- we didn't use the word 'Zionism' even then. Didn't call ourselves Zionists. I think that as a Jew I had to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: But later I-- I-- I-- I-- I learned, I read the Zionist literature. Culture And-- but then I-- I was under the influence of the-- of the leftist organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did-- what-- what was your experience as a Jew in your little town with-- in relation to the Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was actually-- in my little town 2/3 of my life in Europe, I mean, in Poland, I-- I lived in-- I did live in my little town. The rest I did spend in other towns. I used to-- to-- I went to a little town near Jeziory to see [INAUDIBLE] I was in [POLISH], a few-- few places. I-- I-- now when I look back in retrospect, I see that-- I feel that we were actually-- we were second-rate or third-rate citizens then. There were no Jewish-- Jewish clerks in the post office or in the-- somewhere there was no police, there was no-- we had-- we had one Jewish policeman. [? Doiste ?] was a volunteer in the Polish army against the saboteurs. He was an exception.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Jewish people lived, they were-- they were-- they had trades, blacksmiths and tailors and stores. But we didn't have-- people didn't have any jobs, government jobs. So it was-- it was tough to make a living. And on this-- under these circumstances, the many-- there was an organization, the Bund, a Socialist Jewish party. It was very, very influential in Poland, very popular. And that was under-- under the influence of the-- of the literature on Marxism. And then I-- then I-- I would say I was disappointed in all these things. When did you become disappointed? When did you become disillusioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I read the-- the-- the trials in Soviet Russia, I guess. The-- the Trotskyites, all the-- the-- all of the opponents of Stalin. So then I said, must be something wrong with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience acts of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: About that, I remember once with a conversation with my brother, he said there was anti-Semitism in my town. I myself didn't experience. I didn't-- it was not such-- I didn't experience any persecution or something in Poland. But-- but I felt we lived a different life. We were isolated, Jewish people were. We lived-- we-- we-- we spoke a different language. We-- life was based on religious customs and all these things. I forgot, what was the topic? Acts of-- Anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Did you feel that the-- that the isolation was inflicted upon you? Or did you feel that it was self--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that it was self-determined?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people were-- people were polite and we'd actually-- my mother actually was born in a village, and the best-- our best-- when I was about six years old, five years old, our best friend was a Jewish peasant, a Jewish lady-- a non-Jewish lady. The name by [? Jurza. ?] We called her aunt, Aunt [? Jurza. ?] And so my mother spoke perfect white Russian. She was a blond. And she mingled with the-- with the-- with peasant people, with ladies. And so and she was liked by-- by the-- by the population, by the non-Jewish, the peasants. They used to buy the-- what do you-- what do you say it in English? The dye that you-- that you're dying, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was-- there was a very subtle hostility. In school, in the public school, we all sometimes from the-- from the Polish boys, Christ killer. You're a Christ killer. And then let's say, once a year, when they used to go from the church with the ceremony, they called it, you know when the-- the-- birthday of Jesus. What do you call that-- that-- that name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Christmas Day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. So people didn't go out of the-- of the home. They used to cover the-- the-- the icons in the streets. So people used to stay home then. I used to lay out the ground sometimes. It was-- I-- I-- I-- I brought myself to do that. But we knew there was a limit. We can-- we didn't want to provoke them by-- by-- by being seen. So that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the street, on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you or was your mother aware of acts of violence against the Jews? Either--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no violence--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no violence--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in my little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: --against the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in Poland, no, not officially. There was no violence.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about where your mother came from, her little village? Were there acts of violence?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, peaceful. I'd sit with a friend-- a friend, a non-Jewish friend, and the peasants used to buy in my mother's store. But I didn't-- I didn't-- there was no violence. There was no crime, even. Not even-- I don't remember any crime in-- in my little town, even in the Polish population.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- but your sense was that the Jews were second-class citizens, is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. That-- that we felt in our bones. Because we couldn't-- people couldn't have a job-- couldn't have government jobs. The rabbi and the cantor lived from the-- made a living out of the Jewish people, from the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about professional jobs?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- there were--

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no doctors.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were Jewish lawyers and doctors. And in my little town, there was not even one Jewish lawyer or-- or-- or doctor. There were doctors in Poland, lawyers. But it is known in the universities there was anti-Semitism. There used to be not Jews-- Jews used to sit on different-- on different benches. It's almost like the black people in America. That was in the-- in the big town. But in little towns we didn't-- that's interesting people don't know that. My town was an exceptional little town. We were a minority. In my town, Porzecze, the Jewish people were a minority, a small minority. That was the only-- the only little town, shtetl, that I know of with a Jewish minority. In other little towns around Porzecze, the-- the-- the shtetlekh consisted of Jews. Most of them, the Jews were in the town. The non-Jews lived outside the town, the suburbs. But there was one-- one little town, Sidra, where I was teaching, that it was like a Jewish-- like-- like-- like-- I felt like I was in a Jewish land. There were only Jews there. Only once a week they used to come to the market, the peasants, to sell things. But otherwise, we were there. We were used to-- to-- to-- to work down the streets about 11 o'clock, 10 o'clock at night, and only Jews were there. It was actually an autonomy, in a way, in a culture. Even in-- Grodno, in a big town, where gymnasiums, Jewish places, everything was in Hebrew, the schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your teaching when you moved, when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: See, I was teaching privately, even in my little town too, privately. I didn't have formal education as a teacher. And when you asked about [NON-ENGLISH], about future, we didn't think in these terms. Now I'm-- I'm-- I'm reliving that-- that fear, what would I do if I grew up later? I didn't have any-- any profession. Yeah, but when the Soviets came, I became a teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in 1938, I-- I was working in a little-- in that Sidra, in that-- that little town. When I came in the year 1939, the service took over. So I was in my little town already. And then-- and then they opened classes for-- for people who wanted to be teachers. And so I volunteered. And I graduated from my class in six months, a very intensive class. Like ulpan, and the method of teaching was excellent, I must say this, in-- in-- in-- in that-- in that school we learned Russian, Russian geography and biology and-- and-- we learned a lot. Took me six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I chose to be a teacher in-- in-- in-- near Sokolka, in a little village. I wanted to be far from-- they offered me a job in my little town. But I-- I begged them not to send me there because I didn't-- I wanted to be near my Bashe. And they showed me the maps, the little-- little villages where I went to work. So I-- I appointed to that place near her. I was there. I was teaching the whole year. And every Sunday-- we got Sundays to come for the weekend to my-- to-- to her in Sokolka. Cult

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941-- in 1941, I-- I-- I left for vacation to my little town. So when I left that-- that house, I have Bashe there. She insisted I stay her-- with her. And I insisted that she comes with me. So we were about discussing that what to do. And then she accompanied me to the train, and the train, she said to me, and OK, come-- come to me, or come-- come with me. I said, no, come with me. And then the-- the-- that the men said, OK, we are moving. And the train moved, and I went to my little town. And then at-- at night, I had a dream that war broke out. I had a dream that war broke out. And I woke up about-- in the morning, I woke up and Mother came to my bed and said, says, Leybl, [NON-ENGLISH]. She said, [NON-ENGLISH] It's a word. So I went to my-- to my neighbor and I heard the-- the-- the-- the radio. The-- and the radio a voice of Soviet Russia, their representative, that war broke out and Hitler attacked our homeland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel protected by the Soviets, by the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At that time there was no-- there was no transportation. Train didn't run, and there was chaos. And the-- the-- the Russians escaped in big trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any indication that war might break out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We knew that about-- we knew about. And I was-- I was prepared. I knew-- I knew-- I knew that it would be a disaster. Some people didn't believe me. I was-- they said I was a pessimist, they used to call me in my little town. They thought, one of the tragedies of the Jewish people was actually disbelief. Many people, unfortunately, Jewish people, didn't believe that Hitler would do that, that the Germans would exterminate the Jewish people. I was-- I was one of them who did believe. I said-- I said [NON-ENGLISH]. It's bad. It will be-- it will be a year in destruction. Hitler said in And I read-- I heard his-- his-- his-- speeches. He was like a-- like a madman he spoke.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you think that it would be destruction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I knew about, I read about-- when Hitler sent out many Jews from Germany to a little camp [POLISH] in Poland. And then the Jews told-- told the people, the Jewish people, how life in Germany, persecution of the Jewish people. And I even wrote a poem at that time about that, but they never published it. And then I remember when I heard on the radio, I came to my mother, I said, Mom, you had [INAUDIBLE]. I'm running. Told her, I have to go to Soviet Russia. I am running.

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave me bread and some, I think, underwear. And I was-- I left my house. It was in the evening. And I look back, I say, how could I-- how could I know I have to go to Russia? How can I know the direction? But I came to Jeziory, where my family was. And from there, I and my cousin, we-- we left in a group of young people. What was your cousin's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bill, [? Berele. ?] His name was [? Berele. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Borowick also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Borowick.

speaker: interviewee
text: A very talented-- talented young man, very talented. He would have been a scientist now, I would think.

speaker: interviewer
text: This will end our first tape.
